name: Pico de Miravalles y Sierra de Mijedo – circular by the Camino del Norte
name_original: Pico de Miravalles y Sierra de Mijedo – circular por el Camino del Norte
region_intro: Hiking around Noja offers diverse landscapes along Spain's verdant Cantabrian coast. The region is characterized by extensive beaches like Trengandín and Ris, significant marshlands within the Santoña, Victoria and Joyel Marshes Natural Park, and picturesque coastal scenery. These features provide varied terrain for hiking, from sandy shores to marshland trails and cliff-top paths. Best hiking trails around Noja The most popular hiking route is Pico de Miravalles y Sierra de Mijedo – circular by the Camino del Norte, a 5.5 miles (8.8 km) moderate trail that takes 2 hours 42 minutes to complete. This route offers views of the Sierra de Mijedo and follows part of the historic Camino del Norte. Another top favourite among local hikers is Piedra Pombera Beach – Playa de Ris loop from Noja, an easy 3.9 miles (6.3 km) path. This trail provides accessible coastal walking between two of Noja's notable beaches. Local hikers also love the Punta de la Mesa – Piedra Pombera Beach loop from Noja, a 5.1 miles (8.3 km) trail leading through coastal areas and past beaches, often completed in about 2 hours 8 minutes. Hiking around Noja is defined by coastal paths, extensive beaches, and protected marshlands. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy strolls to moderate excursions. The routes in Noja are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 80 reviews. More than 600 hikers have used komoot to explore Noja's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Noja?
A: Noja offers a diverse selection of over 35 hiking routes, catering to various fitness levels. These include 17 easy trails and 18 moderate excursions, ensuring there's something for everyone to explore the region's unique landscapes.

Q: What types of landscapes can I expect to see while hiking in Noja?
A: Hiking in Noja provides a rich variety of scenery. You'll encounter stunning, extensive beaches like Trengandín and Ris, dramatic coastal paths with picturesque views of coves and cliffs, and the ecologically significant Santoña, Victoria and Joyel Marshes Natural Park. The region is characterized by its verdant Cantabrian coastline and unique rock formations visible at low tide.

Q: Are there easy hiking trails suitable for beginners or families in Noja?
A: Yes, Noja has several easy trails perfect for beginners or families. An excellent option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31756845" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Piedra Pombera Beach – Playa de Ris loop from Noja&lt;/a&gt;, an easy 6.3 km path that offers accessible coastal walking between two of Noja's beautiful beaches.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in Noja?
A: Many of Noja's trails are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e998354336/pico-de-miravalles-y-sierra-de-mijedo-circular-by-the-camino-del-norte" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico de Miravalles y Sierra de Mijedo – circular by the Camino del Norte&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 8.8 km circular route offering views of the Sierra de Mijedo. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31614265" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Punta de la Mesa – Piedra Pombera Beach loop from Noja&lt;/a&gt;, a 8.3 km moderate trail.

Q: What are some notable natural attractions or landmarks to look out for on hikes?
A: Noja's hikes are rich with natural beauty. You can explore the unique rock formations at Trengandín Beach, visit &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4136054" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Piedra Pombera Beach&lt;/a&gt;, or enjoy birdwatching at the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5410001" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santoña, Victoria and Joyel Marshes Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;. For panoramic views, seek out the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5389027" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Marshes Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3387497" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Brusco (Mijedo)&lt;/a&gt; summit.

Q: Is Noja a good destination for birdwatching while hiking?
A: Absolutely. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5410001" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santoña, Victoria and Joyel Marshes Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;, a protected habitat of great ecological importance, is recognized as one of Spain's best birdwatching spots. Designated trails within the marshes offer excellent opportunities to observe a wide variety of migratory birds.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Noja?
A: Noja's mild Cantabrian climate makes it suitable for hiking for much of the year. The dramatic local tides are a constant feature, reshaping the shoreline and inviting long walks. Spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures and fewer crowds, while summer is popular for combining hikes with beach activities.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about the trails in Noja?
A: The hiking routes in Noja are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 80 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the diverse terrain, from coastal paths connecting renowned beaches to trails through marshlands and viewpoints, and the options available for different ability levels.

Q: Are there any coastal walks that connect Noja's beaches?
A: Yes, Noja is famous for its coastal walks. The 'Ruta de la Costa' is a popular easy trail that connects the municipality's beaches, providing postcard-worthy views of coves and cliffs. You can also enjoy routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31899033" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Playa de Trengandin – Punta del Aguila loop from Noja&lt;/a&gt;, which showcases the stunning coastline.

Q: Are there any cultural or historical points of interest near the hiking trails?
A: Beyond its natural beauty, Noja town itself features traditional stone mansions and houses, a medieval bridge, chapels, and the Church of San Pedro, built on an ancient medieval monastery. These cultural points of interest can be explored before or after your hikes, offering a glimpse into the region's heritage.

Q: What are the typical difficulty levels and durations for hikes in Noja?
A: Noja offers a good mix of difficulties. You'll find 17 easy routes, often taking around 1.5 to 2 hours to complete for distances up to 6-7 km. There are also 18 moderate trails, which can range from 2 to 3 hours for distances between 8-10 km, requiring a bit more fitness and sure-footedness. There are no difficult trails listed in the area.
tips: If you go down from the direction of Cueto Cabrero towards Punta del Brusco, there is a well-marked trail but with bushes and brambles high above the road and it is difficult to pass without getting the occasional scratch, but just when you begin to descend from Brusco, there are a section (approximately 100 meters) very slippery and steep and completely covered with gravel, it has cost us a lot to go down there (and that was quite dry) because there were sections of trail that could hardly be seen with the gravel (some covered us by above high) and as with steps and very slippery. After such a difficult section, it connects with another path that is clearer and more traveled that comes from Berria beach and, although steep, can be lowered more comfortably. I see this section when it's raining complicated.
Intermediate zone between Cueto Cabrero and Miravalles
To climb the Miravalles peak you have to make a small and easy climb to find the mailbox, but once there the views of the Noja and Buciero area with Santoña at your feet are beautiful.

The National Geographic Institute and Google confuse this Cima del Mijedo and call it Brusco. Both the locals of the two slopes and the closest Institutions, Patronato de las Marismas, the Counselor for Rural Development, nautical charts and historical maps, call this summit as Cima Mijedo, and as such it is marked on the tablets inside the Mount. . The so-called Cueto Cabrero has always been called Brusco, where the Napoleonic ruins of Fort Brusco are located.
An ideal place for those who enjoy bird watching. Very close to the La Llosa urbanization and Trengandín beach, it is a place worth visiting.
